skill.name: go-best-practices
skill.description: Guides Go development with type-first design, explicit errors, small package structure, functional options, configuration validation, and table-driven tests.
skill.summary: Apply type-first Go patterns for clearer APIs, safer errors, and maintainable services.
skill.author: 0xBigBoss

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command, environment access, weak crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found these are Markdown and Go documentation examples. No executable scripts, prompt injection attempts, data exfiltration, or malicious behavior were found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Reported as External Commands
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged Go fenced code blocks and inline backtick text as Ruby or shell execution. These locations are documentation examples only and do not define executable shell commands or scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown fences, inline code, or Go examples in SKILL.md. No shell command invocation, installer script, or runtime execution path exists in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Environment Variable Access in Configuration Example
low_findings.description: The file shows a Go configuration pattern using os.Getenv for DATABASE_URL, API_KEY, PORT, and ENV. This is instructional sample code and does not read or transmit environment variables from the user machine.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The env access appears only inside a Go code sample under the Configuration section. There is no network sink, file write, or live skill code that could expose secrets.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Pattern
low_findings.description: The flagged lines do not contain cryptographic APIs, hash functions, or encryption algorithms. They are the skill description and general guidance about returning errors.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithm usage at the flagged lines. The detected pattern does not match the semantic content.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: The flagged lines contain ordinary prose or Go identifiers such as type system, IDs, and PORT validation. They do not run host reconnaissance commands or collect system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are part of Go examples and explanatory text. No commands such as uname, whoami, ipconfig, netstat, or filesystem inventory are present.

content.value_statement: Go projects often drift into weak types and inconsistent errors. This skill guides agents to use explicit types, interfaces, options, context, and tests.
actual_capabilities: Guides type-first design with structs, custom types, interfaces, and enums.
Recommends explicit error handling with wrapped errors and default switch cases.
Shows functional options for configurable constructors with sensible defaults.
Promotes small package files, package-level APIs, and colocated Go tests.
Provides configuration loading patterns with validation at startup.
limitations: It provides guidance only and does not run Go tools or format code.
It does not include project-specific architecture rules beyond general Go practices.
It does not verify generated code with tests unless the agent runs them separately.
It includes examples, but not a full production framework or library.

use_cases.title: Design Safer Go Domain Models
use_cases.description: Use custom types, structs, and constructors to reduce invalid states before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Review Go Error Handling
use_cases.description: Check functions for contextual errors, explicit default cases, and recoverable failure paths.

use_cases.title: Standardize Service Configuration
use_cases.description: Create a typed configuration pattern that validates required values once at startup.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Typed Model
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new domain type
prompt_templates.prompt: Use go-best-practices to design typed Go structs and constructors for this domain object: [describe object]. Include validation boundaries and error returns.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Function Signatures
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply go-best-practices to review these Go function signatures. Suggest stronger custom types, interfaces, and explicit error behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Configuration Loading
prompt_templates.prompt: Use go-best-practices to refactor this Go service configuration into a typed Config struct with startup validation and clear defaults.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Go Package Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply go-best-practices to audit this Go package. Focus on type boundaries, package structure, error wrapping, contexts, and table-driven tests.

output_examples.input: Design a user creation flow in Go.
output_examples.output: A response that starts with UserID, User, and CreateUserRequest types, then defines CreateUser with validation and error returns.

output_examples.input: Review error handling in a status processor.
output_examples.output: A response that recommends wrapped errors, a default branch for unhandled statuses, and table-driven tests for each status.
A response that proposes a Config struct, required secret validation, development defaults, and one startup load path.

best_practices: Define types and function signatures before implementing behavior.
Return contextual errors and preserve wrapped error chains.
Pass dependencies and context explicitly instead of relying on mutable package state.
anti_patterns: Using raw strings for distinct domain identifiers across a codebase.
Scattering environment lookups through business logic.
Leaving switch branches or unimplemented paths to fail silently.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Go code?
faq.answer: No. It is a guidance skill that influences code design and review responses.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it when writing, refactoring, or reviewing Go files that need stronger types and clearer errors.

faq.question: Does it replace gofmt or go test?
faq.answer: No. It complements those tools by guiding design choices before verification.

faq.question: Can it help with configuration handling?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends typed configuration loaded once at startup with required value validation.

faq.question: Is it specific to web services?
faq.answer: No. The patterns apply to Go packages, services, command-line tools, and libraries.

faq.question: Does it require a particular Go framework?
faq.answer: No. It uses standard Go patterns such as interfaces, context, errors, and table-driven tests.
